one_line_takeaway: AGENTICRED replaces hand-designed red-teaming agent workflows with an LLM-driven evolutionary search over agentic-system code, achieving 96-100% ASR on open-weight targets (vs. 4-88% for AdvReasoning/AutoDAN-Turbo/PAIR/UJA baselines) and up to 100% ASR transfer to several proprietary models including GPT-5.1 and DeepSeek-R1, but the search collapses without a strong meta-agent LLM and a nontrivial seed archive, and shows mode collapse toward known jailbreak techniques.
key_finding: On HarmBench Standard, systems discovered by AGENTICRED reach 96% ASR on Llama-2-7B, 98% on Llama-3-8B, and 100% on Qwen3-8B, versus baseline ASRs of Self-Refine (4/14/58%), AdvReasoning (60/88/80%), AutoDAN-Turbo (36/62/83%), PAIR (-/75/47%), and UJA (-/72/63%) on the same three models -- surpassing JudgeScore-Guided AdvReasoning by 46 points and AdvReasoning by 36 points on Llama-2-7B within six generations. Under the stricter StrongREJECT judge, AGENTICRED scores 0.58/0.59/0.85 (mean +/- ~0.006-0.008) on L2-7B/L3-8B/Q3-8B respectively, versus AutoDAN-Turbo (0.12/0.23/-), PAIR (0.05/0.12/-), TAP (0.04/0.13/-), and PAP-top5 (0.10/0.08/-) -- described as outperforming AutoDAN-Turbo by 300% on Llama-2-7B and 157% on Llama-3-8B. Human-agreement check for the HarmBench judge (200 pairs, 5 annotators): precision 0.9139, recall 0.9324, specificity 0.7500, F1 0.9231, accuracy 0.8850. Cross-benchmark generalization: 100% ASR maintained on AdvBench and ClearHarm using systems tuned only on HarmBench. Transfer to proprietary models (Table 2, baseline numbers taken from Wang et al. 2026): AGENTICRED reaches 100% ASR on GPT-3.5-Turbo, GPT-4o, GPT-5.1, DeepSeek-R1, and DeepSeek-V3.2; 88% on GPT-5.2; 96% on Qwen3-Max; and notably 60% on Claude Sonnet-3.5 and 48% on Claude Haiku-4.5 -- the paper flags these Claude models as 'among the most safety-aligned targets that most attack strategies struggle to crack,' where AGENTICRED still substantially outperforms baselines such as AutoDAN-Turbo (1.0%/0.5%), PAIR (3.0%/13.0%), and X-Teaming (67.9%/47.5%) on those same two models. Ablations: replacing the meta-agent LLM with DeepSeek-R1 degrades design quality; removing JS-Guided AdvReasoning from the seed archive causes the search to stall (no improvement beyond generation 2), showing outcome is warm-start-dependent, not pure emergent discovery from scratch. Qualitative inspection of the top-ASR discovered system shows it reinvented reward shaping, refusal-phrase blacklisting/suppression, prefix-injection, and evolutionary crossover/mutation over prompts -- techniques the authors state emerged without manual programming, though they note this reflects the meta-agent's pretrained knowledge of the red-teaming literature rather than novel techniques absent from prior work.
core_contribution: AGENTICRED (paper title on the PDF is 'AGENTICRED: Evolving Agentic Systems for Red-Teaming'; the loaded metadata record's title 'AgenticRed: Optimizing Agentic Systems for Automated Red-teaming' appears to be a paraphrase/older title) is an automated pipeline that treats red-teaming as a system-design problem rather than an attacker-policy-optimization problem. It uses an LLM 'meta agent' (GPT-5-2025-08-07) to iteratively write new agentic red-teaming systems in code, building on Meta Agent Search (Hu et al., 2025) as its search backbone. The pipeline starts from an archive seeded with two red-teaming-specific baseline systems (Self-Refine/Reflexion, and a new JudgeScore-Guided Adversarial Reasoning variant the authors built), applies evolutionary selection (keeping only the 'fittest' candidate system per generation, evaluated by ASR on a small subset), and maintains generational knowledge (FailedPromptMemory and SucceedPromptMemory) so later generations avoid repeating failed attacks and can be scored for novelty/diversity against prior successful attacks.
threat_model: Black-box, query-agnostic jailbreaking of a target LLM T: given a malicious intent I from a benchmark dataset D, the red-teaming agentic system A generates a prompt p = A(I) (optionally with a target-phrase prefix) such that a judge function J classifies T(p) as satisfying I (ASR = E[J(T(A(I)),I)]). The attacker requires query access to the target model's outputs and to a judge/classifier (HarmBench-Llama-2-13b-cls, later cross-checked with StrongREJECT and human raters); the JS-Guided AdvReasoning baseline component specifically avoids requiring target-model logits (black-box), instead exploiting the judge model's first-token logit confidence. The meta-agent itself (GPT-5) never directly queries the closed proprietary targets during the evolutionary/search phase -- transfer to proprietary models is evaluated only post hoc.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) Systematic mode collapse -- because the meta agent is trained on literature containing existing red-teaming research, discovered systems converge on recurring techniques (e.g., attack templates used 10/10 generations, refusal suppression 8/10, genetic crossover/mutation 10/10 in the Mixtral-vs-Llama2 run), limiting exploration of the broader design space and novel-attack discovery. (2) Archive/warm-start dependency -- removing one seed method (JS-Guided AdvReasoning) causes the evolutionary search to stall after generation 2, meaning results depend on a nontrivial human-designed starting point rather than being fully autonomous from-scratch discovery. (3) Meta-agent sensitivity -- swapping the meta-agent LLM to DeepSeek-R1 substantially degrades performance, indicating the method's gains are partly an artifact of using a strong (GPT-5) system-design model. (4) Static-target assumption -- the paper explicitly notes real target models are continually patched, creating an arms race not modeled here; the authors propose attacker-target co-evolution as future work. (5) Baseline numbers in the proprietary-model table (Table 2) are taken directly from a separate paper (Wang et al., 2026) rather than reproduced by the authors themselves, which is a methodological caveat for that specific comparison.

datasets_used: HarmBench Standard (intent/target pairs, evolutionary search and primary evaluation)
200-pair human-annotated subset for judge-vs-human agreement study
quantitative_results: HarmBench ASR baselines on same models: Self-Refine 4/14/58%, AdvReasoning 60/88/80%, AutoDAN-Turbo 36/62/83%, PAIR -/75/47%, UJA -/72/63%
StrongREJECT judge score: AGENTICRED 0.58 (L2-7B), 0.59 (L3-8B), 0.85 (Q3-8B), vs AutoDAN-Turbo 0.12/0.23/-, PAIR 0.05/0.12/-, TAP 0.04/0.13/-, PAP-top5 0.10/0.08/-
AGENTICRED outperforms AutoDAN-Turbo by 300% (Llama-2-7B) and 157% (Llama-3-8B) under StrongREJECT
Judge-vs-human agreement (n=200, 5 annotators): precision 0.9139, recall 0.9324, specificity 0.7500, F1 0.9231, accuracy 0.8850

Proprietary-model ASR (Table 2, HarmBench): AGENTICRED 100% on GPT-3.5-Turbo, GPT-4o, GPT-5.1, DeepSeek-R1, DeepSeek-V3.2; 88% on GPT-5.2; 96% on Qwen3-Max; 60% on Claude Sonnet-3.5; 48% on Claude Haiku-4.5
Mode-collapse counts in Mixtral-vs-Llama2 run: attack templates used in 10/10 generations, refusal suppression in 8/10, genetic operators in 10/10
100% ASR maintained on AdvBench and ClearHarm cross-benchmark transfer
